region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore, East Asia, and Western markets)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general scallop food product class; no specific boiled-scallop subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Scallops — boiled or steamed preparation; mapped under molluscs category in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJL covers scallops as a mollusc; boiled preparation facet applied as closest defensible match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for boiled scallop as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of boiled scallop is approximately 85 g (about 5–6 medium scallops), providing roughly 94 kcal and 17.5 g of protein.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled Scallop — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled scallop (F03050008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15152 (scallops, mixed species, steamed) for completeness.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for boiled scallop based on analogous low-carbohydrate shellfish; University of Sydney GI Database consulted — no direct GI value published for scallop.
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database does not publish a GI value for boiled scallop. Scallops contain approximately 5.41 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly as glycogen, which has a very low glycaemic impact in shellfish. An estimated GI of 5 (low category) is assigned by analogy with other low-carbohydrate shellfish and molluscs, consistent with the negligible postprandial glucose response expected from glycogen-based carbohydrate in seafood. Glycemic load per serving (85 g) = (5 × 4.60) / 100 = 0.23, rounded to 0.3. This is a conservative analogous estimate; no peer-reviewed GI trial for boiled scallop was identified.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 667 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit boiled scallop portions to 50–60 g per serving to keep absolute sodium intake below 400 mg per meal, avoid adding salt or soy sauce, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar, low carbohydrate (5.41 g/100 g), and very high protein (20.54 g/100 g) support stable postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled scallop is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible sugar content and high protein slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose rise — avoid high-sugar dipping sauces or sweet glazes that would raise the glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.09 g/100 g) and low total fat (0.84 g/100 g) are cardioprotective, but high sodium (667 mg/100 g) poses a blood-pressure risk relevant to cardiovascular outcomes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled scallop's lean lipid profile and modest omega-3 content are broadly favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (667 mg/100 g) requires portion control to 60–80 g per serving and avoidance of additional sodium-containing condiments to prevent adverse blood-pressure effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (667 mg/100 g) may exacerbate RA-related tissue oedema; scallops provide modest omega-3 fatty acids but are not a rich anti-inflammatory source.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit portion size to 60 g to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention; pair with fatty fish such as salmon or sardines to supplement omega-3 intake for stronger anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled scallop (HPB code F03050008) is a minimally processed seafood product consisting of whole scallop meat cooked by boiling in water until the flesh is tender yet firm. Scallops are bivalve molluscs prized for their naturally sweet, delicate, slightly briny flavour and their smooth, succulent texture. Per 100 g, boiled scallop delivers 111 kcal, 20.54 g of high-quality complete protein, only 0.84 g of total fat (of which 0.09 g is saturated), 5.41 g of carbohydrate (predominantly glycogen, with zero free sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and a notably high sodium content of 667 mg. Potassium is 314 mg/100 g, calcium 10 mg, iron 0.58 mg, and cholesterol 41 mg. The high protein density and negligible sugar make boiled scallop an excellent food for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. However, the sodium level of 667 mg/100 g exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-safe foods, making portion control essential for individuals with high blood pressure or cardiovascular disease. The boiling preparation method avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat dry cooking such as frying or grilling. Boiled scallop is widely consumed across Singapore, East Asia, and Western markets as a snack, appetiser, or protein component in main meals. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and considered halal. Common pairings include light soy-based dipping sauces, lemon, garlic butter, or steamed vegetables.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in boiled scallop?
Is boiled scallop high in sodium?
Is scallop good for high blood pressure?
Is boiled scallop low in fat?
Can I eat scallop if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03050008) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15152 (scallops, mixed species, steamed) as a supporting secondary source. Source match type is exact for the HPB primary record.
notes: 1. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB database — boiled scallop contains no free sugars; carbohydrate is present as glycogen. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero — scallop is an animal-derived mollusc with no plant cell-wall fibre. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central cross-reference for steamed scallop. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB primary source or USDA cross-reference for this preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record F03050008 and were not available in the USDA FoodData Central steamed scallop entry (NDB 15152) at the level of detail required for this preparation. 3. Glycemic index is set to 5 (low category) as a conservative analogous estimate; the University of Sydney GI Database does not publish a GI value for boiled scallop. The carbohydrate present (5.41 g/100 g) is predominantly glycogen with negligible glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per serving (85 g) is approximately 0.3. 4. Saturated fat (0.09 g), monounsaturated fat (0.07 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.22 g) values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15152 (scallops, mixed species, steamed) as the HPB record does not report fatty acid breakdown. 5. Halal status: scallops are generally considered halal under the Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence widely followed in Singapore; kosher status is false as shellfish are not kosher. 6. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact boiled-scallop subclass exists in either ontology as of 2026-04-16.
